data.title: Adobe: Undue Fear Makes It A Strong Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have covered Adobe Inc (NASDAQ:ADBE) a few times in the past and have maintained a consistently bullish tone. When OpenAI's video generator Sora was released (causing a selloff in Adobe), I wrote that investors should ignore the FUD. I rated the company a Hold at the time based on a pretty rich valuation but felt that the market was overestimating the impacts of Sora on Adobe. Next, I upgraded Adobe based on what I called Very Compelling AI Monetization In Q2. The company has increased prices for products that include its Firefly AI model. Meanwhile, Adobe GenStudio is penetrating the enterprise market as a full-stack Martech platform. This will move Adobe further up the value chain and result in more pricing power over time. That's why the recent Deutsche Bank downgrade was puzzling to me.

When new technologies begin to proliferate consumer markets, investors immediately begin debating second and third order effects to predict the path certain stocks may take. The unquestionable winners of the first wave are the chip designers, foundries, server OEMs, and other names within the hardware ecosystem. The market is also focused on the expected winners of the second order effect of commercial AI: software.

AI will greatly improve the capabilities of software and be a disruptive force. You can't argue against that. However, we can't know with any sort of certainty what impacts we'll see in the medium-to-long term. What we can say for certain is that the market won't get it entirely right. There will be overhyped losers that don't pan out and long-term winners that get wrongfully punished in the short run. I believe this is the case with Adobe, as it has been getting punished on the prospect of AI greatly reducing the need for its software. Analysts are also concerned with monetization efforts.

As a result, Adobe has underperformed quite significantly this year. While management is very enthusiastic about the pace of product innovation, analysts are focused on the slowing pace of revenue growth. Despite this, Adobe's strategy is sensible. Tomorrow's winners will be dictated by who proliferates the market most effectively today and builds an entrenched user base. Adobe is a very clear leader in this sense, but the market is punishing the stock because these efforts have not yet translated to an acceleration in sales growth. The company now trades at a very low forward multiple relative to historical averages while the company has been cannibalizing its shares in recent years.

The last time the company's forward multiple was this low, it quickly mean-corrected in the preceding months. Since then, the company has also reduced shares outstanding by over 5%. This weakness creates an exceptional opportunity to accumulate stock in an incredible company.

Adobe's AI Opportunity

Many armchair prognosticators have said that "AI won't replace your job, someone using AI will". This is right and wrong. AI will definitely displace some jobs. It will also create new jobs and turbocharge productivity in others. The sentiment of this quote holds true for enterprises too, which is the reason I remain bullish on Adobe. AI won't replace software companies, rather software companies that most effectively integrate AI into their stack will eat the lunch of those that don't. This is the case for Adobe.

Adobe offers the most extensive set of software products for digital creators. Countless websites are built on AEM, Adobe Experience Manager, which dictates the style, placement, and look-and-feel of web components. Photoshop has become synonymous with photo editing. Students learn to be digital creators in Adobe products and experts rely on it for their daily needs. Movie producers rely on Adobe's video editing software. Marketers depend on its full stack marketing software that offers targeting, analytics, and content deployment services. Any consumer or enterprise that needs high quality creative software is dependent on Adobe for a full suite of creative software. No company has the suite of products to match this, so a company that wants to avoid Adobe will have a piecemeal set of software products to achieve their needs. This adds undue complexity and erodes efficiency. It just makes sense to build on Adobe, and AI won't be changing this. The Adobe Flywheel is as strong as ever.

Despite the company's strong current moat, it can't afford to sit still as AI proliferates. And it hasn't. The company has weaved Firefly, a family of Generative AI models, into its entire stack. Firefly surpassed 16 billion token generations in the most recent quarter, with innovative features like Generative Fill gaining traction with users. There's a slew of other features like text-to-image, text-to-video, scene-to-image, and audio translation.

AI is set to power the next generation of digital creation and Adobe is focused on providing the underlying AI infrastructure for enterprise marketing with GenStudio. The rise of digital advertising has necessitated an intense focus on "Personal and Relevant" marketing. Marketers have better insights on their target audiences than ever before, but the key is to use these insights to deliver the right content to the right person at the right time. Delivering highly relevant, individualized content allows enterprises to enhance marketing efficacy. Adobe is critical to achieve this, which has resulted in continued double digit compounding. In Q4, the company achieved $3.3 billion in Digital Experience revenue, which grew 11% YoY Net new Creative Cloud ARR was $405 million.

The company has shipped AI products at an excellent pace in both the Digital Experience and Digital Media segments. Within Digital Media, which is mostly the Adobe Acrobat PDF reader, the launch of an AI assistant is revolutionizing how people interact with digital documents. It allows people to directly talk to legal contracts, marketing documents, digital applications, and the countless other use cases for PDFs. AI Assistant conversations doubled QoQ in Q4. Document Cloud revenue reached $843 million, growing 17% YoY and net new Document Cloud ARR reached a record of $173 million. The company ended the quarter with an ARR of $3.48 billion, growing 23% YoY. The demand for AI features within Adobe's ecosystem is clear.

Adobe has been a leader in commercializing AI, AI research, and monetizing AI. Firefly was one of the first family of models readily available for users at scale, the company has an extensive R&D program, and Firefly features have a very transparent and reasonable pricing structure. AI is going to dramatically increase efficiency for creative professionals, the effects of which will be most pronounced for people that use Adobe software. It isn't going to drive people away from Adobe, rather the increased efficiency could increase demand for Adobe's AI-powered software. Such is the Jevons Paradox.

In short, I think the market is dead wrong here. The recent weakness in the stock has brought the valuation to an extremely reasonable level relative to historical averages, based on way overblown fears.

AI Fears, The Risk of Demand Erosion

The core of investors' fear toward Adobe is that AI will severely erode demand for digital content editing services. Anyone that has used OpenAI's image generator DALL-E or video generator Sora understands this risk. These products greatly enhance the layman's ability to create digital media, but it doesn't eliminate Adobe products from the market outright.

Enterprises that have built their entire digital ecosystems atop Adobe's building blocks can't just unwind this. They are locked in for the foreseeable future. A company whose website is built on AEM components, or a company that uses Adobe's Martech stack to power their digital marketing operation, or a company that uses PDF's extensively every day won't just eliminate their Adobe contracts because OpenAI's DALL-E can easily create cool photos. That's just nonsensical.

Still, analysts on the Q4 call were dubious of Adobe's monetization efforts. Alex Zukin of Wolfe Research asked about how AI will contribute to Digital Media ARR growth. David Wadhwani, President of the Digital Media segment, said that AI enhancements are "expanding our user base, increasing the value of core subscriptions, and creating entirely new enterprise revenue streams." In Q4, monthly active users grew over 25% YoY, surpassing 650 million paid and free users. This was mostly driven by strong top of funnel momentum through Acrobat Web, with monthly active users up nearly 50% YoY. The adobe.com domain saw record traffic in Q4. Driving this traffic was demand for Adobe Express, a suite of free software products, which acts as a phenomenal top-of-funnel vehicle to get new users introduced to the Adobe ecosystem.

Meanwhile, Keith Weiss of Morgan Stanley asked the most pointed question to management (emphasis added):

Keith Weiss (Morgan Stanley)

"A lot of excitement from you guys on the conference call about the pace of innovation... but apologies to state the obvious, investors aren’t feeling that excitement. The stock is a major underperformer year-to-date, and it's down again after hours today.

Despite all this innovation—new monetization avenues, pricing going up—the growth is going in the wrong direction. Is there some leak in the bucket? Is there share loss? Is there something that we’re not seeing that is taking away that momentum?"

Shantanu Narayen (CEO, Adobe)

"Keith, I understand the question, and I think if we take a step back and look at our full-year execution, we guided at the beginning of the year, then again mid-year, and then again at Q3, and in each case, we beat those targets. So, from our perspective, we are executing very well.

I think the question specifically around Digital Media net new ARR and Creative Cloud growth—one nuance I’d highlight is that Creative actually performed better than what might be evident at first glance. Acrobat’s contribution to Creative Cloud ARR increased, which means if you strip that out, the non-Acrobat creative products saw higher growth than what’s reported.

As we move forward, GenStudio will be a significant unlock for the enterprise, because it brings everything together in a way no other competitor can. AI in Firefly Services, custom model training, campaign automation, and brand-consistent content generation are already seeing strong demand.

Now, as to why growth isn’t accelerating even faster—AI is a multi-phase opportunity. First, we prioritize proliferation—getting AI deeply embedded into workflows. Once users are dependent on AI for creative work, monetization kicks in through new tiers and premium offerings. We’re confident this transition will translate into accelerating financial growth."

Management isn't directly focused on monetizing AI yet. This is the right strategy. Proliferation will win the day because users will become more deeply entrenched into Adobe's ecosystem. Usage will grow and full-stack workflows will create a very strong lock-in effect and a compelling flywheel for the company. At that point, it can focus on monetizing these users and compounding sales for years to come.

Investor Takeaway

Adobe has a superb moat, illustrated by its world-class margin profile of 89% gross margin and a 25% net margin. While its current moat makes the Adobe castle impenetrable, the company is not resting on its laurels. Management has demonstrated its willingness to respond to major technological shifts throughout the AI revolution so far. Even though many pessimists believe AI will displace Adobe software, or are worried about the lack of growth acceleration, the company is powering full steam ahead to supercharge its products with AI tech.

This will contribute to continued consistency in sales growth over time, which flows through nicely to profit growth. The company has compounded sales in the double digits for 10 consecutive years and the sudden proliferation of more efficient methods of digital content creation will not stop this. Recent fears have led to a very low multiple relative to historical averages, around the multiple that the stock fell to in the post-COVID correction and the inflation-driven bear market. Both times, the stock swiftly rebounded as the company continued to execute with excellence at scale. This time is no different. Adobe is a Strong Buy.

data.title: Hermès: New Geographies And Non-Leather Categories Support A Strong 'Hold'
data.description:
Hermès (OTCPK:HESAF) (OTCPK:HESAY) is a one of a kind company. The strongest evidence to support this argument was clearly shown in the past two years, as Hermès maintained mid-teens growth during a tough period in luxury, with almost all of its peers struggling.

The recently announced results are another example of Hermès' superiority, with organic growth accelerating to 18%, with relatively stable margins.

The only question is, valuation. Let's dive in.

Introduction To Hermès

From the day it was founded in 1873 by Thierry Hermès, the French house has become the epidemy of luxury. Still owned by the founding family, Hermès has grown to become one of the world's most renowned luxury brands, with iconic designs like the Birkin and the Kelly bag.

Since its early days as a harness workshop, Hermès continuously expanded into new categories like handbags, clothing, silk, watches, beauty, fragrance, tableware, and more.

Hermès is not just a luxury perfectionist, it's one impeccable business as well, ending 2024 with over €15.1 billion in sales, and €4.6 billion in annual profit. Unsurprisingly, Hermès has been one of the best performing stocks since its IPO, and over the past decade.

In a series of articles, I delved into Hermès' unique growth strategy, its unparalleled strengths and consistency, and mostly maintained a bullish view.

However, I downgraded the stock to a 'Hold' recently, for the sole reason I thought its valuation reached a level it's no longer attractive. Well, the stock continued to march higher, but the company also delivered better-than-expected results.

So, let's see where we stand today.

Impeccable Fourth Quarter Results To Cap Off A Remarkable 2024

Hermès ended 2024 with €15.1 billion in sales, up 13.0% Y/Y, or 14.7% at constant exchange rates. For the quarter, sales were close to €4.0 billion, marking the highest growth rate for the year at 17.7%.

Impressively, Hermès maintained gross margins higher than 70%, and operating margins above 40%, despite headwinds from foreign currency and less pricing action. This resulted in EPS of €43.9, up 7% from 2023.

Looking at geographies, Middle East continues to be a hyper growth story, once again doubling Y/Y, and it's likely to surpass €1.0 billion in annual sales by 2026. Besides China, all regions saw double-digit growth, reflecting global strength. And, in China, we saw an uptick in Q4, which might suggest an improvement in the consumer environment in the all-important region for Hermès.

Looking at sectors, we saw a big dispersion between the "core" categories, including Leather, Ready-To-Wear, and Home (Other Hermès), and the smaller categories, which had lower growth rates. This is one worry I had after Q3, as it reflects Hermès having to lean on its most-desired products (and those that require the most focus on scarcity).

However, Q4 marked a meaningful improvement on that front, with growth meaningfully accelerating across all segments, setting the stage for a strong start to 2025 as well.

So, Hermès put a close on another strong year, but this time it was even more impressive in light of results from peers like LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF), and even Brunello Cuccinelli (OTCPK:BCUCF), which grew 1%, -12%, and 12%, compared to Hermès with 14.7%.

Non-Leather And New Geographies Show Significant Growth Runway

Hermès seeks to maintain scarcity, especially with its most expensive and thought-after items. Scarcity and 'quiet luxury' is what makes Hermès so resilient, there's essentially a waitlist to buy its products.

This is one reason why Hermès can grow its sales rapidly without opening too many more stores. All it has to do is "lift the lid" a little bit, and more sales will come. In 2024, Hermès generated over €58 million in annual sales per store.

The problem is, though, that the more you sell from a given store, the harder it is to maintain scarcity, especially if you don't expand into new categories and items. This is why it is critical, in my view, that Hermès does exactly that.

On the category front, we can see that Leather Goods grew slower than total revenues, with growth being led by Ready-to-wear & Accessories, Watches, and Other Hermès Sectors (mainly home products and Jewelry). Meanwhile, Silk & Textiles, and Perfume & Beauty, were also strong grower.

On the geographic front, we can also see a declining exposure to Asia Pacific (mostly China), while Europe, Americas, and the Middle East, which are large and diversified regions, growing in share.

In my view, this means that although Hermès is now almost the size of Kering in terms of sales, there's plenty of room for them to grow, across geographies, and product categories, while expanding their footprint.

The Valuation & Intentionally Limited Growth Problem

Building on the previous discussion, I think it's safe to say that if Hermès could press a button and fulfil 100% of its demand, it could have tripled its sales, perhaps even more. However, Hermès is dedicated to artisanal, hand-made craft. Such quality can't be mass manufactured, and the paradox is that if Hermès did fulfil all its demand, then it would lose a lot of it.

Hermès had significant capacity investments in recent years, opening its 23rd leather goods workshop in September 2024, and expanding perfume, beauty, and tableware sites. It's currently working on three workshop projects to be done one each over the next three years.

This, combined with price increases and very strong utilization, has led to the remarkable growth we're seeing, but we should not expect this level of growth going forward. This is just not the Hermès way of doing things. As such, ~10% revenue growth is where analyst estimates stand, and what I believe we'll see on a normalized basis going forward.

As such, Hermès' valuation of roughly 59 times forward earnings cannot be justified by its growth trajectory. Rather, it is only justified by the unparalleled quality, resiliency, and consistency, of its business.

However, even for Hermès, this valuation is beyond historical peaks, which gives me pause. I don't think investors can count on much more multiple expansion at this point. In fact, I think it's more likely the multiple will contract over the long term, just because a multiple close to 60x isn't sustainable.

Conclusion

The way I see it, the best case scenario for Hermès is one where it maintains the 60x multiple, rises in line with earnings growth, and continues to pay a nice dividend.

While this is a market-beating scenario, there's always the risk of multiple contraction from these elevated levels. I mean, would a 50x multiple be insulting? I don't think so.

As such, I reiterate Hermès at a 'Hold', specifically because multiple expansion and contraction is essentially impossible to predict and therefore to rely on.

data.title: Enbridge: Why I Prefer The Preferreds Again, Downgrading Common
data.description:
2024 Plays Out As Expected

Last quarter, I upgraded the common shares of Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) (TSX:ENB:CA) to Buy from Hold. At that time, I was satisfied that the company had developed a good "pipeline" of growth projects in Liquids Pipelines, Gas Transmission, and Gas Distribution that would support 3-5% growth in distributable cash flow. At that time, the shares were yielding around 6.5%, nearly at parity with the US dollar denominated Series L preferred (OTCPK:EBBNF) (TSX:ENB.PF.U:CA) and about 50 basis points below my two buy-rated USD preferreds, Series 1 (OTC:EBBGF) (TSX:ENB.PR.V:CA) and Series 5 (OTCPK:EBGEF) (TSX:ENB.PF.V:CA). With the current yields that close, the prospect for 3-5% distributable cash flow (DCF) growth made it more likely that the yield on the common shares would surpass the yield on the preferreds within a few years. Additionally, the preferreds pay a resettable rate that resets every 5 years based on the yield of the 5-year US Treasury note (US5Y) 30 days before the reset date. Investing in the preferreds carries the risk of a future dividend cut if the rate resets lower.

Since that last article, not much has changed with respect to Enbridge, the company. The 4Q results presentation shows basically the same set of growth opportunities. As for the financial results, they turned out about as I predicted a year ago. Added EBITDA from the US gas utility acquisitions was offset by added interest expense and share dilution from the debt and cash issued to finance the deal. As a result, full year 2024 adjusted EPS was just one Canadian penny better than the 2023 result. DCF per share was just slightly better with 1.5% growth.

Enbridge common stock has been more surprising than the company since last quarter. It is up over 14% since the start of November, which is 10-13 percentage points better than the USD preferreds.

With the outperformance in the common share price, the yield spread has narrowed with preferreds. Also, a "higher for longer" scenario appears to be playing out for US Treasuries. This lowers the risk of a downward reset in the preferreds. It now looks like any of the three USD preferreds have a chance of beating the common shares for total income delivered over the next six years. Let's check the outlook for Enbridge and the math on the income to see why.

Enbridge Outlook

Enbridge is guiding for DCF per share in the range of $5.50-$5.90 (Canadian) in 2025. At the midpoint, that is 2.4% growth from the 2024 result of $5.56. The company has also set its dividend at $0.9425 quarterly, or $3.77 for the year. This is 3% growth from the 2024 payout off $3.66. The company further predicts DCF growth of 3% in 2026, improving to 5% in 2027 and beyond.

There could be upside risk to this plan from data center expansion and electrical demand growth, something Enbridge might not have even been thinking about when they offered to buy the US gas utilities. Enbridge is serving this demand in the US directly through solar projects with power offtake agreements with Amazon (AMZN) AT&T (T), and Toyota (TM). Still, it will be hard to build enough renewables and nuclear power sources fast enough to satisfy the anticipated electrical demand, so natural gas is the next best fuel from the standpoint of these buyers, who all have CO2 reduction targets. With cooperative state governments and electric utilities eager to work with Enbridge in Ohio, North Carolina, and Utah, the company can supply the gas to meet the growing need for power generation.

With growing discussion about tariffs from the US directed at Canada, it is worth discussing the subject as a downside risk. This risk looks minimal for Enbridge, though. All of the company's segments outside of the Liquids Pipeline business have flows which are mostly in-country. Within Liquids Pipelines, the Mainline has a huge cross border flow. However, the Great Lakes area refineries on the end of the mainline have little choice but to run Canadian crude. Most pipelines that brought crude north from the gulf coast in the past have now been reversed, such as Capline and Seaway. The Great Lakes refineries can still bring WTI crude in from Texas and Oklahoma, but they can't run 100% on light sweet crude, which would underutilize the coking capacity these refineries have installed over the years to upgrade heavy oil. Even with a tariff, the refineries will still fully utilize the mainline.

Refineries on the gulf coast have more optionality on crude sourcing, with the ability to bring in crude by tanker. They did this in greater volumes as recently as 15 years ago, before northbound pipelines like Seaway were reversed. Tariffs on Canadian crude could prompt some marginal switching to waterborne imports, reducing flows in pipelines like Seaway and ETCOP where Enbridge has partial ownership. I would not expect these impacts to be significant or long-lasting if they happened.

With these risks in mind, it looks reasonable to forecast 3% dividend growth in 2026 and 5% in 2027 and beyond, in line with the company's DCF forecast.

Income Math

Now we can compare Enbridge common stock to the three USD preferreds. Since my last article, the yield spread has widened, with the preferreds opening up an income advantage over the common. Series L (OTCPK:EBBNF) now yields 30 basis points more, Series 5 (OTCPK:EBGEF) yields 79 bp more, and Series 1 (OTC:EBBGF) yields 90 bp more.

That's not the end of the story, of course. We also have to consider growth in ENB common shares as well as resets on the preferreds. With the higher price in ENB common since my last article as well as the higher 5-year Treasury rate, growth in the common share dividend is not enough to deliver more income over the next 6 years to beat any of the preferreds on total income paid. This is a change from my last article.

Please note the key assumption listed in the table on the reset rate. It assumes current 5-year Treasury rates are in place on each reset date. If you think rates are going to be significantly lower, all of the preferreds will look less attractive. Persistent inflation and a higher for longer rate scenario now appear more likely than they did just a few months ago, but they are by no means guaranteed.

As we approach the first reset date in 2027, the relative attractiveness of the preferreds to each other is changing. Series L has the lowest coupon of the three preferreds but the highest premium to the 5-year Treasury in its reset formula, as well as the nearest reset date in 2027. If interest rates stay at their current levels or increase, Series L goes from the lowest to the highest dividend payer.

Series 1 is in the middle with a reset date 9 months after Series L, a higher current yield, and an almost identical premium in the reset formula. Assuming constant treasury rates, Series 1 gives you more income in the first three years, compensating for the later reset to higher payouts.

Series 5 has the lowest premium to the 5-year Treasury, as well as the latest reset date. While the current yield is close to Series 1, it pays the lowest of the three preferreds over the 6-year period.

Conclusion

Enbridge remains a solid operating company with slow but steady growth. However, given the runup in the common share price the past few months, I would wait for it to pull back before starting or adding to a position. The three US dollar denominated series of preferred shares have opened up a greater yield spread relative to the common. The spread is wide enough now that even with the growth of the common dividend, all three preferreds would produce more income over the next 6 years, given constant 5-year Treasury note yields.

Series 1 and Series 5 have the best current yields at 80-90 basis points above the common shares. However, Series L has the highest premium to the 5-year Treasury in its reset formula and the nearest reset date. That makes Series L more attractive as we approach the reset.

At the end of the day, this analysis hinges on what the 5-year Treasury note is actually yielding 30 days before each respective reset date. Investors who believe a return to the low-rate environment of 2010-2020 is likely should avoid resettable preferreds altogether. Those who think rates will stay the same or go higher will find resettable preferreds to be a possible hedge against the impact of inflation and higher rates on their other holdings.

With the probability of a higher for longer interest rate scenario increasing, I now have the following ratings on the 4 Enbridge securities discussed here:

  • Common shares - Downgrade to Hold
  • Series L - Upgrade to Buy
  • Series 1 - Maintain Buy
  • Series 5 - Maintain Buy

data.title: 1 Main Capital Partners Q4 2024 Fund Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

In the fourth quarter, 1 Main Capital Partners, L.P. (the “Fund”) returned (2.8)% net, bringing its year-to- date return to 8.2%. This compares to full year returns of 25.0% and 11.5% for the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) and Russell 2000 (RTY) 1, respectively. Since its inception nearly six years ago, the Fund has delivered an annualized net return of 20.9%, compared to 13.1% and 6.5% for the SPX and RTY, respectively. Accordingly, a $1 million investment at inception would be worth $3.7 million today, after all fees and expenses. The same amount invested in the SPX or RTY (with dividends reinvested) would be worth $2.3 million and $1.5 million, respectively.

The Fund’s primary objective is to generate strong long-term performance, without taking on excess risk. To accomplish this, our capital has historically been concentrated in my best ideas, causing us to look and act differently than the indexes. Of course, being different means that there will inevitably be periods where we underperform a strong market, especially when the strength is narrow.

2024 was such a year of underperformance. While the businesses we own largely made good progress growing their collective values, their share prices lagged. The biggest detractor for the year was IWG, which cost us approximately (4)%. At one point, its shares were up double digits before ending down 17% (in USD terms). In the 2.5 years we have held this investment, the business has grown substantially and generated cash, but the stock has barely appreciated. As our largest holding for much of this period, the opportunity cost has been real. However, the risk / reward has gotten significantly better coming into 2025 (more on this later) and I believe we will be well compensated for our patience with this investment.

The next largest detractors were ENZ and SUP, which together cost us an additional (4)%. Unfortunately, our ENZ losses are unlikely to be fully recovered. When making this investment, I overestimated the speed and ease with which the company would wind down and sell itself, while underestimating the cash it would burn in the interim. I believe its shares are undervalued at current prices but, with diminished upside and our reduced exposure, even a good outcome won’t be sufficient to have made this a worthwhile investment. This special situation turned out to not be so special after all.

Fortunately, I remain optimistic regarding our opportunistic investment in SUP. As one of the largest manufacturers of passenger vehicle wheels in NA and Europe, the company suffered from a substantial decline in light vehicle production – initially at the onset of the pandemic, then due to auto supply chain disruptions and associated inflation. Despite the lack of a full volume recovery, SUP has successfully generated cash, refinanced maturing debt, and relocated manufacturing to cost competitive locations. Furthermore, SUP’s 2025 financial results are likely to improve even without industry volume growth, given that its 2024 revenue and margins were depressed by the relocation of its European production from Germany to Poland. While SUP remains a high-risk holding, the potential upside here is significant.

As I look at the Fund’s portfolio, I believe we are as well positioned for strong prospective absolute performance as at any point since its 2018 inception. Prices of our holdings will follow approximate value over time, and we largely own a collection of companies that are cheap, cash-generative and growing. As the largest partner in the Fund, and with nearly all my net worth invested alongside you, I am optimistic about the future.

Market breadth

Market breadth was narrow in 2024, with gains largely driven by the largest US mega-caps. As such, while the SPX generated a total return of 25% for the year, the equal-weight S&P 500 (SPW) returned just 13%.

Accordingly, the number of US equities outperforming the market has fallen to a near historic low of ~30%, the lowest since the 90s.

Furthermore, the ten largest companies in the SPX now represent almost 40% of the index, a historic level.

Of course, this level of concentration happens when the valuations of the largest companies are significantly higher than smaller companies. Accordingly, the forward P/E multiple of the top ten is approaching 30x, a level significantly higher than multiples of the S&P 490, and small and mid-cap indexes.

Interestingly, as the SPX has become more concentrated, investors have also been allocating an increasing portion of their capital into SPX-linked passive strategies. While momentum is a powerful factor, it has inevitably led to significant concentration in portfolios.

The continued shift of assets from active to passive strategies is noteworthy. As I noted in the Fund’s Q3’23 letter, nonfundamental flows (NFFs) could have a significant impact on stock prices, and this shift to passive is very much an example of a NFF.

In a world where passive strategies make up a large but stagnant share of investments, asset prices should be very efficient. After all, a small pool of highly-talented active managers should be much better at setting marginal prices than a large pool that has many less-talented stock pickers. However, price distortions can occur as money is being pulled from active strategies and allocated to passive indexes, since this leads to cheap stocks (however defined) being sold and expensive stocks bought. Thankfully distortions also create an opportunity for us to capitalize on mispricings. Importantly, I continue to invest our capital in companies that grow earnings at attractive rates, such that we can absorb fluctuations in multiples while still generating ample returns.

Current positioning

At quarter-end, the Fund’s top five positions remained Basic-Fit (OTCPK:BSFFF), Caesars Entertainment (CZR), dentalcorp (DNTL:CA), International Workplace Group (OTCPK:IWGFF) and Water Intelligence (OTCPK:WTLLF). Together, these holdings accounted for approximately 60% of capital.

An update on International Workplace Group

IWG is the Fund’s largest position. While its stock price is just marginally above our cost, the company’s business has improved significantly since our initial investment 2+ years ago and I remain optimistic that it will deliver a strong IRR. Recently, I joined my friend Andrew Walker on Yet Another Value Podcast to provide my latest thoughts on the investment.

As a reminder, IWG is the global leader in flexible office space (coworking), with $4 billion of system sales across 4k+ locations in 120+ countries. The company was founded in 1989 by CEO Mark Dixon, who still owns 25% of the company.

Historically, IWG owned or leased nearly all its locations, while reinvesting its cash flow into new units. However, the company has recently seen a significant acceleration in demand for its managed workspace concept. In this model, landlords fund the buildout of new locations while IWG collects a recurring management fee for operating the space, typically ~15% of revenues.

Currently, managed and franchised locations (M&F) account for just 15% of IWG’s system sales and the segment is approximately breakeven. However, by the end of the decade I expect M&F to grow substantially, to nearly half of system-sales, and generate EBITDA of $500 million. Assuming this is directionally correct, the M&F segment alone will be worth many multiples of IWG’s current market value.

Even if my optimism around the M&F business turns out to be misplaced (which I believe is unlikely), IWG will probably still be a good investment. In 2025, the company should generate almost $300 million of free cash flow just from its non-M&F businesses: owned and leased locations and Worka. These segments alone likely justify a valuation 2x the current share price.

While EBITDA and FCF have grown substantially since we first invested in IWG, the shares have barely moved. This has created a great setup for the stock. Specifically, 2025 will be the first year where IWG reports GAAP financials instead of lease-distorting IFRS. The company will also reach its target leverage ratio, allowing it to buy back shares for the first time since the pandemic. This is happening just as M&F revenues are accelerating and profitability is inflecting. With the company trading at a near all-time low valuation, right as financials improve and as the company turns on its buyback, I believe the setup for our investment is quite attractive.

Outlook

Despite cooling significantly from the 2022 highs, inflation has remained sticky, particularly in services. At the same time, consumer spending, the backbone of the US economy, has become increasingly constrained by higher rates and tighter credit conditions. Accordingly, the Fed is now in the unenviable position of having to choose between fighting inflation or supporting economic growth. I have long held the view that it will prioritize fighting inflation. Additionally, the Fed’s predicament is compounded by federal deficits, which means that government spending must eventually be reduced – another headwind to growth. Given this backdrop, I believe economic growth is unlikely to meaningfully accelerate in the near term.

At the same time, investors must deal with the added uncertainties of a potential trade war(s) and reduction in undocumented labor force, both of which are being pursued by the incoming US administration. Together with higher interest rates, one would expect business confidence and investment to be challenged.

However, the headwinds that come with higher interest rates, tariffs and reduction in labor force may be offset by several mitigating factors. Specifically, Scott Bessent, who was recently appointed as Treasury Secretary for the incoming Trump administration, has outlined his 3-3-3 plan, which aims to generate 3% real GDP growth, reduce the fiscal deficit by half to 3% of GDP, and boost U.S. oil production by 3 million barrels per day. Bessent has also suggested that he is highly focused on achieving favorable 10-year interest rates rather than pursuing low short-term rates.

To accomplish this, Bessent and the new administration are pursuing a policy of aggressive deregulation, which could lead to improved productivity and increased private investment – especially aided by the ongoing development of AI technologies. Additionally, the newly created department of government efficiency (DOGE) is attempting to reduce the size of government which, if successful, could free up labor to work in the private sector. Lastly, lower energy prices feed through almost every part of the economy.

As I have stated in the past, macro is tough to predict because conditions aren’t static. Policymakers, employees, consumers and investors constantly adjust their behavior to any new environment and are all generally working to grow the economy over time.

However, despite my not taking strong macro views, I constantly attempt to position the Fund’s portfolio by taking into consideration any obvious challenges. For example, I have purposefully been avoiding exposure to businesses that are overly dependent on low-cost labor, low interest rates, or imports from abroad. Lastly, our holdings are largely well capitalized, such that they can play offense when a slowing economy inevitably forces competitors to play defense.

As usual, I love what we own and am looking forward to seeing what the future has in store. As a reminder, the Founder’s Class remains available to partners who have at least $5 million of capital in the Fund, until AUM reaches $100 million. Please reach out with any thoughts or questions.

Thank you for your support and confidence.

Sincerely,

Yaron Naymark

data.title: Denny's: Starting 2025 On The Wrong Foot
data.description:
ICR Conference Optimism Obliterated

It's been a hard day's night. I told you FY 2024 was a dog year for America's once-favorite Denny's (NASDAQ:DENN). I was skeptical once when they touted their 'C-R-A-V-E Strategy' and again when they sold a 'Banda Burrito'-based recovery and Keke's as the new fast-growing restaurant chain on the block. Well, it wasn't too hard to buy that story. In the eyes of many, Denny's was cheap at $7! Nothing beats a dose of reality.

Don’t think that this restaurant chain has never been in this tough spot before. Those who have been following restaurant stocks for a while may remember that Denny’s parent company filed for bankruptcy in 1997! But don’t think that the Grand Slam restaurant chain isn’t in trouble. They’re everywhere you look, you just have to open your eyes. In fact, I wrote an extensive article explaining why most family dining chains are having trouble capturing new guests. I highly recommend reading it.

But what scared the market was not the full results for FY 2024, but rather how Denny's started the year. And I can tell you that it was not on the right foot. Let's take it easy. What did we have until the ICR Conference? Basically the in-depth 'C-R-A-V-E Strategy', a system-wide AUV target, an incentive program for remodeling and many promises for the 'Banda Burrito' that by then was already present throughout the system. Now they had a plan, okay. But what temporarily changed the mood was:

  1. Same-store sales had shifted direction for both Denny’s and Keke’s. To recap, we previously had data for franchises and company-owned restaurants, but now we have it in full. They grew 1.2% year over year for Denny’s franchised restaurants and were flat for company-owned restaurants. At Keke’s, same-store sales for franchised restaurants increased 1.2% year over year, while company-owned restaurants declined 3.7% year over year. Keep in mind that Denny’s had spent most of FY 2024 with its same-store sales declining in the low single digits. Keke’s, on the other hand, was declining in the mid single digits; and
  2. Keke's, its more modern daytime dining concept, began to gain more market attention after reporting strong ASR and accelerating opening commitments to approximately 140 locations to open by FY 2028. In fact, many analysts credited Keke's for its performance, which far outpaced Denny's.

Backed by an upgrade from Citi and more recently Wedbush, Denny's shares rose 25% to $7. Immediately after the earnings report, the wings were clipped and the stock price fell more than 20% to $5. Ouch, that hurt!

But rest assured. My mission here is to show you what happened and whether $5 is a bargain for this dog. Just look at the recent momentum of Papa John's (PZZA), the ugly duckling of the 'Battle of the Pizzas', and you can see that there is a lot of money to be made in undervalued restaurant stocks.

Everything We Didn't See in the Preliminary Results

During its preliminary results, Denny's reported its same-store sales for each concept. Of course, it did not break this down between franchises and company-owned stores.

Here I've put together a chart that breaks down same-store sales for each concept, as well as compares them to some peers in the Family Dining segment:

The funny thing is that Denny's got most of its same-store sales growth for Q4 for one reason: average check growth. And before you think it was just price increases, let me tell you it was part of their cross-selling strategy. I had already considered it back in August when they launched their '$2, $4, $6, $8 Menu'.

If same-store sales for Denny's grew 1.1% year-over-year when we combine franchises and company-owned stores, the average check growth was 6.5% year-over-year! That's right, you read that correctly.

We can draw two conclusions from this:

  1. Even without disclosing it, the mix has increased significantly. At least in the low single digits year over year. This is important, as many restaurants are struggling to contain the mix as guests are choosing increasingly cheaper items in their orders; and
  2. Traffic fell 5.4% year over year, outpacing declines seen at comparable companies like Cracker Barrel (CBRL) and First Watch Restaurant Group (FWRG).

Now, like any good old barbell-style value promotion, Denny's new platform did a good job of generating incremental dollars by encouraging guests to order a full plate (either in the $6 or $8 price range) and add an additional item to the main course, which could be ordered in the $2 or $4 price ranges. We saw this also happen with Jack In The Box (JACK) in mid-2024, when they added 'Munchies Under $4' to the QSR value war. The result was quite similar. Even though it generated weak traffic, the promotion was still able to generate incremental dollars by complementing larger orders like the Smashed Jack.

As for Keke's, even though the company is not disclosing the details, we can guess that traffic is not as bad as it seems. However, the biggest headwind for its same-store sales was Hurricanes Milton and Helene. With over fifty locations in Florida, both hurricanes impacted its comps by at least 110 basis points. That is, had they not happened, Keke's would have seen comps grow 4.1% year over year during Q4. Not bad for a company that saw same-store sales decline 4% year over year less than two quarters ago.

The Hangover of 2024 Could Be Felt in Early 2025

Things start to get a little more complicated when we get into the Q1 FY 2025 issues. But before I start with the bad news, let me tell you some good news. But make no mistake, there aren't many.

Keke's has already opened three restaurants this year. They've finally made it to their seventh state, Georgia! Keke's January same-store sales grew 6.2% year-over-year. A great result despite them being flat in February.

Well, let's start with the bad news. Keke's has terminated two franchise agreements, which has impacted the operation of eleven restaurants. This represents more than 25% of Keke's entire footprint! Of these, only two will close permanently, as the rest will be divided between refranchising, remodeling, and reintegration into the company's portfolio.

Turning to Denny’s, management identified a severe deterioration in same-store sales for January that led to a 0.7% year-over-year decline (0.8% decline for franchises and 1% increase for company-owned restaurants), primarily in the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic regions. But the worst was in February. During the first two weeks of this month, Denny’s reported a staggering 5% year-over-year drop in same-store sales! We haven’t seen a drop like that in all of FY 2024.

Note that the consumer deterioration alleged by Denny's did not occur at Keke's, which says more about the brand than necessarily the consumer environment. Now, the momentum may have cooled at the end of Q4 as it actually did in December. But I find it very difficult to argue that there was a consumer retraction of this size.

To top it off, the company released weak and vague guidance:

  • They expect same-store sales to be between negative 2% and positive 1% for all of FY 2025, which suggests that the situation could worsen further this year;
  • More renovations and a new loyalty program; Opening 25 to 40 Denny's restaurants, as well as closing 70 to 90 restaurants. Now, that would give us a decline of thirty restaurants in the best-case scenario and sixty-five restaurants in the worst-case scenario;
  • Of the 13 to 20 Keke's openings year-round, about 60 percent of them will be company-owned;
  • An adjusted EBITDA of $80 million to $85 million; and
  • $15 million and $25 million in share repurchases.

Now that you understand the size of the problem, let's get down to business and see if it's cheap or not!

Valuation: Updating My PT

Without further ado, let's start by looking at their multiples.

A Comps Model using comparable companies like Dine Brands seems appropriate to me since comparing it to other restaurant chains would be like comparing apples and pears. It just wouldn't work. Denny's is in serious trouble, and what better way to compare it than to another ugly duckling?

  • Using an estimated P/E for FY 2025 of 4.52 and an EPS of $0.48, we find a PT of $2.17;
  • Starting from an EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.39, estimated top-line sales of $470.78 million and a simplified capital structure with approximately 50 million shares outstanding (assuming full-year buybacks), we arrive at a PT of $5.24;
  • Considering a P/S of 0.5, top-line sales of $470.78 million and shares outstanding of 50 million, we arrive at an RPS of $9.41. Now, that would give us a PT of $4.71;
  • Considering an EV/EBIT of 9.55, top-line sales of $470.78 million, EBIT margins of 11%, and all the assumptions we made for EV/EBITDA, we find a PT of $4.69.

IMO, going here is not the right path. Denny's looks cheap, but take a second look and you'll see that it doesn't. In fact, with the stock falling, it looks fairly valued based on the new earnings estimates.

It turns out that management has already scared investors with the bad news in January and February. Note that this was the opposite of what they did at the ICR Conference. So, anything they report for Q1 FY 2025 that isn't catastrophic could push the stock back up to the $6-$7 range. Either way, I wouldn't put my money on it, just watch. I wouldn't want to be stuck in this trade for the rest of my life. So, I'll continue to watch the wheels turn. I'm maintaining my 'Hold' rating, although I'm more bearish, I believe the market has already priced in the bad news.

data.title: Lululemon: Encouraging Earnings Revisions, Raising My Price Target
data.description:
The consumer keeps on spending. Friday’s Retail Sales report was disappointing (though it was likely due to bad weather), but the latest card-spending data from Bank of America point to no major let-up in consumption. In fact, final demand outperformed in the Q4 GDP report despite other components that led to a somewhat soft quarter of overall economic activity.

We’ll hear from a slew of retailers in the coming days as the Q4 earnings season comes closer to the finish line. Lululemon (NASDAQ:LULU) doesn’t issue its quarterly results until late March, though.

I had a buy rating on LULU back in November 2024, anticipating that recovery into year-end would unfold after volatility in the preceding months. Today, I reiterate a buy rating, even after a 26% rally since my previous analysis. On the heels of a solid Q3 report and with solid macro tailwinds, I am raising my intrinsic value estimate and will call out new key areas on the chart to monitor.

Risks are apparent, though, considering that the rate of credit card loan delinquencies is now at the highest level since 2012, according to the recently released NY Fed Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit.

Solid January Card Spending per BofA

January Retail Sales Weak: Core Control Group Lowest Since February 2022

Rising Credit Card Delinquencies

Back in December, LULU reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 GAAP EPS of $2.87 topped the Wall Street consensus estimate of $2.72 while revenue of $2.4 billion, up 9% from a year earlier, was a small $40 million beat. Shares soared by 15.9% in the session that followed – its best earnings-day reaction in at least the last three years, per data from Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS).

Driving the impressive quarterly profit were healthy sales numbers internationally. The now $47.5 billion market cap company tallied 33% ex-US growth with particularly strong numbers in Canada. Margin expansion was a bright spot, too, as was the management team’s solid cost-control efforts. Not surprisingly, LULU lifted its full-year EPS guidance to be in the 8-10% range, even with a shorter holiday-shopping window.

Overall, North American sales were about stable thanks to modest US revenue changes. China was also robust, with an expected 30% annual sales climb. Its Board of Directors authorized an additional $1 billion share repurchase program.

After the impressive Q3 report, the firm issued a preliminary earnings announcement at the ICR retail conference in January. On January 13, LULU noted that it expects Q4 revenue to be in the range of $3.56-$3.58 billion, above previous estimates. The firm guided diluted EPS to be in the range of $5.81-$5.85 for the quarter. Shares rose once more after that news hit the tape.

On the earnings outlook, analysts have turned more bullish on LULU’s prospects. EPS is seen rising by 13% in FY 2025 with a high single-digit growth rate in the out year through FY 2027. Revenue growth is expected to be at about the same rate as the bottom line, so I see some upside to earnings if operating leverage gains can be had – solid margins would support that.

Profitability trends are quite strong today considering the company’s trailing 12-month free cash flow per share of $12.77. The sell side is quite upbeat about LULU, too, as there have been a high 30 earnings upgrades in the past 90 days, compared with just a lone downgrade.

Lululemon: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, with an improved outlook since last year, and as the management team appears to be back on track with trends and execution, I am lifting my forward EPS assumption from $14.75 to $15.50. Keep in mind that LULU is already into its FY 2026 (its FY 2025 ended on Feb 2, 2025). Also, I’m willing to raise the P/E multiple assumption from 24x to 27x based on the solid guidance and healthy international sales. The 5-year average P/E is near 43x.

Applying those figures, I arrive at an intrinsic value target of $419, making the stock still undervalued today. Shares do trade expensive to its sector peers, but LULU is well below its long-term valuation multiples.

LULU: Expensive to the Industry, Cheap Relative to its History

Key risks include uncertain consumer trends, adverse currency changes, and macro headwinds if we see weaker US and international economic growth.

At the firm-specific level, keeping up with competitors and smaller niche retailers could be a challenge, and recent weakness from health and beauty companies is not a great sign for LULU’s customer base.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Thursday, March 20 AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares sufficiently undervalued in my estimation, LULU’s technical situation is mixed but generally solid. Notice in the chart below that shares have rallied sharply from their 2024 lows. The stock touched $226 at the August nadir, but then went on to nearly double to a high of $423 earlier this year. A trading range has emerged between $367 and that peak, so we are in a bit of a wait-and-see situation ahead of the next profit report. What’s more, there was a negative RSI divergence at the 2025 high, which portended the modest drop to under $400 just recently.

But take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average – it’s now positively sloped, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. I see upside resistance at the March 2024 downside gap near $480 while support is seen near $345 – the earnings gap from the last report. Finally, with a high amount of volume by price under today’s level, there should be support if we see a protracted pullback.

LULU: Earnings Gaps Tell the Story, 200dma on the Rise

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on LULU. I see the Canadian Apparel, Accessories, and Luxury Goods industry company as modestly undervalued with a decent chart heading into its March earnings report. The management team proved its execution resiliency in the latest quarterly report, and the upbeat preliminary earnings update was encouraging to begin the year.

data.title: MetroCity Bankshares: Lackluster Valuation As Well As Earnings Outlook
data.description:
Earnings of MetroCity Bankshares, Inc. (NASDAQ:MCBS) will likely grow slightly this year on the back of very low loan growth. Further, the average margin will likely be higher this year compared to last year, even though I’m expecting the margin to remain unchanged from last quarter’s level. The higher average margin will support the bottom line. Therefore, I’m expecting the earnings per share to grow by 3% to $2.58 in 2025. The stock is currently trading above the year-end target price. Further, it is offering a moderate dividend yield of 2.9%. Hence, I’m adopting a hold rating on MetroCity Bankshares.

Margin Likely to be Stable this Year

Historically, MetroCity Bankshares’ net interest margin has moved inversely to market interest rates.

A major reason why MCBS’s margin expands when interest rates dip (and vice versa) is that the average loan yield is quite sticky, while deposit costs move more freely. The loan yield’s stickiness is attributable to the loan composition, which is heavy on real estate loans. These loans normally carry fixed rates over the term of the loan. Residential and commercial real estate loans, altogether, made up 97% of total loans at the end of December 2024.

However, the management also uses derivatives to manipulate the rate sensitivity. MetroCity Bankshares’ net interest margin jumped up in the second quarter of last year, even as the Fed funds rate remained unchanged during the period. The following quote from the second quarter’s 10-Q filing gives the reason for the sudden jump.

The increase in our net interest margin is primarily the result of our decreasing deposit costs from the benefit received on our interest rate derivatives, as well as the continued increase to our loan yields.”

Currently, the Fed seems to have paused interest rate cuts. As there is a lot of uncertainty related to tariffs and retaliatory tariffs and their impact on inflation, I’m assuming for now that the Fed funds rate will remain unchanged this year. As the margin has historically been inversely proportional to interest rate changes, I believe a stable interest rate this year should keep the margin stable too.

Although I’m expecting the net interest margin to remain unchanged from the fourth quarter’s level, the average margin for 2025 will be higher than the average margin for 2024 by around 10 basis points because the margin shot up in the second quarter of last year. The margin of 1Q 2024 was quite low, which is why the average for this year will be higher, even with no sequential change.

The Condition of Georgia’s Residential Market Suggests Loan Growth Will Remain Low

MetroCity Bankshares’ balance sheet ballooned in size from 2020 to 2022. During this period, the loan book grew at a compounded annual growth rate of a massive 38%. Since 2023, the growth has stagnated, with the loan book growing by just 2.7% in 2023 and 0.5% in 2024.

MetroCity Bankshares' subsidiary, Metro City Bank is quite small with just 20 branch locations in 17 towns and cities of Alabama, Florida, Georgia, New York, New Jersey, Texas, and Virginia. I’ve made the following map to visualize MetroCity Bankshares’ locations given on its website.

As is evident from the map, the company’s operations are concentrated in Georgia. The state’s unemployment rate has ticked up since last year, but it’s still quite low. Additionally, currently, the slope of the coincident index is pretty much the same as it was last year, which suggests that economic activity is as good as before.

The economies of MetroCity Bankshares’ most other markets have slightly worsened over the last one year, as depicted by the inching up of the unemployment rates in most states.

The bulk of MetroCity Bankshares’ loans are in the residential real estate segment.

As shown below in the chart taken from the St. Louis Fed’s website, new housing permits in Georgia have been lower over the last two years compared to the years immediately following the pandemic.

Also, Georgia’s house price index has gotten flatter recently compared to the early part of 2023.

All in all, I think the loan growth will remain in the low-single-digit range this year. I’m expecting a loan growth of 1% in 2025. I’m also assuming that MetroCity Bankshares will be able to fund its loan additions through deposits, so there will be very little movement in borrowings.

Expecting Earnings to Rise Slightly to $2.58 per Share

The higher average net interest margin this year and slight loan growth will help lift earnings. However, operating expenses will naturally rise in tandem with revenue growth, which will limit earnings growth. Meanwhile, the provision expense for loan losses will likely remain low like in the last two years because loan additions will be subdued. Further, I’m assuming the non-interest income will remain steady from the last quarter’s level.

Overall, I’m estimating an EPS of $2.58 for 2025, up by just 3% year-over-year.

Total Expected Return is Negative

MetroCity Bankshares’ raised its quarterly dividend twice last year. Since the IPO in 2019, the company has raised its dividend every year (source: external investments website). However, I’m not expecting a dividend hike this year because my earnings estimate and the current quarterly dividend of $0.23 per share suggest a payout ratio of 36%, which is considerably higher than the last five-year average of 28%. The current DPS and the closing market price of February 13 suggest a dividend yield of 2.9%.

I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples to value MetroCity Bankshares.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with my EPS forecast gives a target price of $25.6.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $32.0.

The average price target for December 2025 comes out to $28.8, which suggests an 8.8% downside from the February 13 closing price of $31.54. Adding the dividend yield to the price downside gives a total expected return of negative 5.9%.

I think the total expected return isn’t bad enough to warrant a sell rating. As a result, I’m adopting a hold rating on MetroCity Bankshares.

data.title: NatWest: Medium-Term Earnings Set To Remain Elevated
data.description:
NatWest Group (NYSE:NWG) produced another set of reasonable figures covering its fourth quarter. While financials were arguably a little so-so versus consensus, the market has been playing catch-up here recently, with the outlook for the bank's earnings improving throughout last year.

This has also driven a solid performance for the stock. Prior to today's open, NatWest's ADSs had returned around 17% since my last update in October, outperforming the wider European financials space as well as close peer Lloyds Banking Group (LYG).

Last time out, I upgraded NatWest back to 'Buy' even though the shares were trading for ~1.2x tangible book value. That may not have looked cheap for a European bank, but as I said above, the outlook for NatWest's earnings has been improving – to the point where a premium to tangible book value was more than justified. Given earnings look set to remain strong over the next few years, I remain bullish here, and I leave my 'Buy' rating in place.

Another Beat In Q4

In its fourth quarter, NatWest generated £1.25 billion of profit attributable to ordinary shareholders, beating consensus by around 40%. This mapped to a strong 19% return on tangible equity, which was roughly 530bps above consensus. For the full-year, the bank's return on tangible equity was 17.5%, roughly 150bps above the average sell-side estimate.

Although the headline beat looks strong, trends under the hood were slightly more mixed. Positively, revenue was better than expected, with income of £3.83 billon beating consensus by around 3%. Of this, £2.97 billion came from net interest income ('NII'), which was ~1% ahead of consensus and around 13% higher than the year-ago period.

Like many banks, NatWest uses interest rate swaps to manage its interest rate risk – and this hedging program is a major reason I have been bullish here. While many European names are seeing NII roll over, growing hedge income is still powering NII growth here. Moreover, expectations for U.K. interest rate cuts have been moderating while the yield curve has been steepening – both of which are positive for NatWest's revenue outlook.

Around 20% of NatWest's hedge rolls off each year. Because maturing swaps were written when interest rates were very low, reinvestment is driving material yield pick-up and income growth. This is going to ongoing tailwind for the bank over the next few years, as shown in the slide below.

There have been headwinds as well. Lending margins are one example, with fierce competition in the U.K. mortgage market leading to thin spreads over the relevant swap rates. Even so, hedge dynamics are a major reason why analysts expect NII to continue growing here. Furthermore, those expectations have been increasing. For instance, consensus for FY25 NII is now around £1.3 billion (or ~12%) higher than it was at the start of last year, while consensus FY26 NII is up around £1.8 billion (~15%) in that time.

Costs were a little more mixed last quarter. Operating expenses came in at £2.27 billion, exceeding consensus by nearly 6%. While most of the miss was driven by more volatile litigation/conduct charges, core expenses still missed as well. Moreover, FY25 cost guidance of £8.1 billion was also a shade higher than pre-results consensus.

This drove an in-line print on the pre-provision profit line, with operating profit before impairment of £1.56 billion basically matching consensus. So, while there was a large bottom-line beat, a significant part of this was driven by lower loan loss provisioning. This line came in at £66 million last quarter, around £135 million lighter than analysts expected, mapping to a cost of risk of just ~6bps of customer lending. Lower-than-expected taxes also contributed positively.

Credit quality looks very resilient. Stage 3 loans were still only 1.4% of total lending at the end of last year, which is around 30bps below the bank's 2018-2023 average. And despite the much-feared impact of higher interest rates, mortgage arrears remain in line with recent historical levels, albeit they have been ticking up from the 2022 low point.

Looking ahead, FY25 guidance looks pretty strong to me. At the mid-point, revenue is basically seen in line with pre-Q4 consensus, while I have already mentioned that cost guidance is slightly higher. On the flip side, provisioning guidance is slightly better. Weighing all that up, the 15%-16% return on tangible equity that management expects NatWest to earn this year is roughly where analysts are at as well.

I described 2025 guidance as strong for two reasons. First and foremost, a mid-teens ROTE is objectively good in itself. Secondly, although guidance is by-and-large in line with pre-results consensus, consensus has been playing catch-up here. Compared to the start of 2024, for example, consensus for the bank's 2025 return on tangible equity has increased by ~270bps.

Offering A Double-Digit Total Yield

Relative to year-end tangible book value of 329p per share (roughly $8.30 per ADS), NatWest's current share price equates to a multiple of 1.29x. This is up roughly 0.1x from prior coverage. Given the improved outlook for earnings, I think this move is more than justified – and it still only equates to a P/E of ~8x.

NatWest also offers investors attractive capital returns potential. Consensus has FY25-FY27 cumulative net income at around £14 billion. Given management's 50% dividend payout ratio, this should map to around £7 billion in total FY25-FY27 dividends. Consensus has penciled in a further ~£4.3 billion in buybacks over this period, with this implying a total payout ratio of around 80%. Based on NatWest's current market-cap (~£34 billion), this means the bank could return around one-third of its value to investors over the next three years – with this implying a double-digit annual total yield throughout this period.

Conclusion

NatWest closed out 2024 by delivering better-than-expected fourth quarter results, albeit the bottom-line beat was driven heavily by lower-than-expected loan loss provisioning and lower taxes. Compared to last year, 'higher for longer' interest rate expectations and a steeper yield curve have driven an improvement in the bank's earnings outlook, with both management and the market now expecting the bank to earn a strong mid-teens return on tangible equity this year. Given the positive implications for NatWest's valuation and capital returns potential, I remain comfortable with my previous rating despite the stock's strong returns to date.

data.title: How The U.S. Treasury Can Cash In Big Using Its Gold Revaluation Account
data.description:
The U.S. Treasury can draw up to $700 billion in new funding from its gold revaluation account at the Federal Reserve. And the Treasury could invest this "new money" in a sovereign wealth fund (increasing the money supply by an equal amount).

Using its gold revaluation account would emphasize gold's strength versus the dollar, something the United States government has been trying to downplay for decades. This turn of events would be bullish for gold and weaken the dollar.

Introduction

I started writing about central bank gold revaluation accounts (GRAs) to show how they can use a trick to write off assets, such as government bonds, from their balance sheet with new money from their GRAs. The German central bank even wrote to me in an email that it doesn't rule out this possibility for the future.

Then I discovered how central banks have been using their GRAs to absorb losses, like the Central Bank of Curaçao and Saint Martin (CBCS) did in 2021. Coincidentally, as interest rates went up in 2022 and central banks globally experienced shortfalls, officials from the Dutch and German central banks commented that their massive GRAs underline the soundness of their balance sheets. Providing a solvency backstop, if you will.

Because of how modern central bank balance sheets are structured, GRAs can also be used to fund their respective Treasuries. Even the World Bank noted my writings, as it referenced my research on GRAs in a handbook for asset managers on why to invest in gold.

What Is a Gold Revaluation Account, and How Can It Be Used?

A gold revaluation account (GRA) is an accounting item on the liability side of a central bank balance sheet that records unrealized gains of gold assets.

When the price of gold denominated in fiat currency rises, as it inevitably does in the long run, gold assets increase in value, and concurrently, the GRA swells.

As a formula:

GRA = present gold market value - gold purchasing cost

In this example, central bank X once bought gold for $300, and the present value of that gold has gone up to $1,500, creating a GRA of $1,200. Capital and the Gold Revaluation Account is the central bank's equity.

In many textbooks, you will read GRAs are meant to cushion a decline in the price of gold, but because gold is scarce and the denominator (fiat) on central banks' balance sheets is not-visible in large GRAs of banks that own gold for a long time-there are far more creative possibilities for it.

In the example below, I have illustrated that a central bank can transfer entries from its GRA to its own capital position and absorb losses (+200); to the government's checking account held at the central bank (+100); or to write off assets (-500).

It's just numbers.

If central bank X draws $800 from its GRA, there is still (1200 - 800) $400 left to cushion a potential decline in the price of gold.

The American Setup

Take notice that the central bank of the United States owns not one ounce of gold. With the Gold Reserve Act of 1934, the Federal Reserve transferred all its monetary gold to the Treasury in return for a special series of gold certificates valued at $35 an ounce. These gold certificates are purely an accounting item and can't be redeemed for gold.

Because the U.S. has always been reluctant to raise the statutory price of the gold in its books to downplay the strength of gold versus the dollar, the Fed's gold certificates today are still valued at 42.22 dollars an ounce, a price approved by Congress during the demise of Bretton Woods in the early 1970s.

On February 3, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order for the creation of a sovereign wealth fund within one year. Standing next to him when he signed the order was Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who told reporters, "We're going to monetize the asset side of the U.S. balance sheet for the American people."

What asset can that be? He could be referring to selling off federal lands or vacant government office buildings, but the Financial Times speculated on February 7 that it's a reference to gold:

Some hedge fund contemporaries of Scott Bessent, the hedgie-turned-US Treasury secretary, are speculating about a revaluation of America's gold stocks.

This week, such chatter intensified after Bessent both pledged to "monetise the asset side of the US balance sheet" - in other words, to focus on assets as much as liabilities - while also promising to lower 10-year Treasury yields.

Perhaps the Trump administration does want to draw on the Treasury's gold revaluation account at the Fed, as happened in 1972 when the official gold price was raised from $35 to $38, and in 1973 from $38 to $42.22 an ounce.

Doing so would allow the Treasury to spend hundreds of billions of dollars without increasing the national debt.

Step one would be for Congress to approve an increase in the official price of gold. Step two is for the Treasury to issue new gold certificates for the Federal Reserve.

Once the value of the Fed's gold certificates on its balance sheet goes up, the Treasury's checking account at the Fed, commonly referred to as the Treasury General Account (TGA), increases by the same amount.

As the Fed doesn't own any gold, a windfall from the revaluation of its gold certificates is for the owner of the physical gold, which is the Treasury. Note that when GRAs are used for spending, it creates new money and thus has to be done through central banks. Welcome to the wonderful world of accounting!

The below illustration is from a paper by Albert E. Burger, published by the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis in 1974: "The Monetary Economics of Gold." When, in 1972, the statutory price of gold was revalued to $38 from $35 an ounce, the TGA increased by roughly $800 million.

H/t John Paul Koning. The same procedure is described in the Financial Accounting Manual for Federal Reserve Banks, published in April 2024 (page 12).

From A. E. Burger:

On May 15, 1972, the Treasury took steps to monetize the increased value of the gold stock. This occurred as follows: the Treasury issued to the Federal Reserve Banks gold certificates equal to the increased official dollar value of the gold stock and, in return, the Treasury received from the Federal Reserve an increase of an equal amount in its deposits at the Federal Reserve Banks [TGA].

Following the revaluation of the U.S. monetary gold in 1972, the Treasury spent $800 million on the private sector-without selling an ounce of gold-increasing the monetary base (reserves) and broad money supply (deposits) by an equal amount.

How Much Can Trump Spend?

Currently, the Treasury owns 8,133 tonnes of gold valued at $42.22 an ounce, which equals $11 billion, while the prevailing market value of the gold is roughly $750 billion.

The Treasury can deplete its gold revaluation account via the Fed in full but then runs the risk that the free market price of gold falls below the new U.S. statutory price, which would undercollateralize America's balance sheet.

So, either Trump draws less than $750 billion from this trick, or he takes it all and puts a floor under the free market price of gold, which brings us one step closer to a gold standard. This, of course, would be bullish for gold as its role in the system would be promoted to the highest tier once again.

The Federal Reserve could counter the Treasury's expansion of the monetary base by selling bonds, but this would drive up interest rates, which is not what Trump wants. Trump, firmly in charge since in office, is thought to want a weaker dollar to boost exports.

Expanding the monetary base by drawing on the United States' GRA would do just that and allow him to invest in a sovereign fund. Two birds with one stone.

Sources:

Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (2024): Financial Accounting Manual for Federal Reserve Banks

Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (1974): The Monetary Economics of Gold

Financial Times (2025): Gold glitters as the unimaginable becomes imaginable

Myrmikan Capital, LLC (2025): Gold Revaluation

Nieuwenhuijs, Jan (2022-2024): German Central Bank Doesn't Rule Out Gold Revaluation, Governor Dutch Central Bank States Gold Revaluation Account Is Solvency Backstop, How a Central Bank in the Caribbean Recently Used Its Gold Revaluation Account to Cover Losses, German Central Bank: Gold Revaluation Account Underlines Soundness of Balance Sheet, How Central Banks Can Use Gold Revaluation Accounts in Times of Financial Stress, World Bank Report Highlights Advantage of Central Bank Gold Revaluation Accounts

Reuters (2025): Trump orders creation of US sovereign wealth fund, says it could buy TikTok
