data.title: Upwork: Impressive Profit Growth, But The Longer Term Is Shaky
data.description:
Though we've likely tired of hearing AI-related headlines by now, the harsh reality is that the rapid advance of AI technology is going to impact a sea change in our modern workforces within the next few years. And for many companies, that represents a tremendous opportunity to right-size corporate headcount and manage costs: but for others, AI is a very disruptive force.

To me, Upwork (NASDAQ:UPWK) is one of the companies that stands to be disrupted. The freelance work marketplace has executed tremendously well so far, and its recent Q4 earnings print helped to extend a ~50% rally in the stock that has taken place since October. But the question for investors now is: can the good times last?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Upwork in November, when the stock was trading at similar ~$16 levels. Since then, Upwork stock is attempting a new breakout rally on the back of very strong Q4 results, which showcased the company's profitability chops following its decision to lay off a good chunk of its employees last October. And yet, given Upwork's fair valuation and the longer-term risks that AI poses, I remain neutral on Upwork's prospects.

Understandably, investors are excited about the potential for AI growth here, and Upwork has gone to great lengths to demonstrate that it's a beneficiary of the trend. In Q4, the company reported that total GSV (gross services volume, or the total dollar amount of jobs booked on the Upwork platform) from AI-related projects rose 33% y/y.

The company has also released its own proprietary AI tool, branded "Uma," which it describes as a "work companion" that helps make smart search and recommendations (it seems that almost every software company has released some version of an AI tool like this, often billed as either a companion or a copilot). The company also made a small tuck-in acquisition of a company called Objective that specializes in AI native search.

And yet to me, long-term risks abound. Upwork may be touting huge 30%+ growth rates in AI GSV - but at the same time, its revenue growth has decelerated to the single digits, well below the ~20% growth clip that Upwork had enjoyed at the height of the pandemic, when companies were hybridizing their workforces and relied on freelance or contract labor to fill missing holes.

To me, this suggests that while it's true that Upwork is winning AI-related jobs, it may also be losing a lot of the simpler jobs that are its bread and butter (think of jobs such as editing, web design, translation - many of these functions either can today or eventually will be replaced by AI). Current data suggests that AI is not a net growth driver for Upwork.

With this in mind, I continue to remain on the sidelines for Upwork: while the company's current profit expansion is quite impressive, it's unclear whether this strength can last.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Upwork's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Upwork's total revenue grew only 4% y/y to $191.5 million, though this beat Wall Street's expectations of $180.4 million (-2% y/y) by a huge margin. Still, we note a number of disappointing factors: revenue growth decelerated sharply from 10% y/y growth in Q3. Second, the company's GSV also declined -7% y/y to just $992.8 million, which was worse than a -3% y/y decline in GSV in Q3. Again: how can the company tout AI as a major growth driver, when its growth rates have only slipped since AI began revolutionizing the workforce?

Unsurprisingly, the company blamed its weakness on macro conditions. The company nots that the staffing industry is sitting at multiyear lows. Per CFO Erica Gessert's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

And when macroeconomic conditions improve, these catalysts will be additional growth drivers for us. As we head into 2025, our outlook reflects our caution with regard to the current macroeconomic environment.

While some of the indicators we watch for our business, such as the Jolts report and other macro data showed signs of stabilization in Q4, they remain at multi-year lows. Historically, these indicators tend to have a six to nine-month lag effect on our business. The current macro uncertainty makes us cautious about the outlook for 2025, but despite that Upwork continues to perform well and we are executing across every area of our business."

To me, however, this is hopeful thinking. While it's true that macro conditions have impacted enterprise budgets, some of the staffing reductions that companies are making may prove to be permanent, especially if AI proves to be a reasonable replacement.

We do, however, appreciate Upwork's major strides in profitability amid tougher top-line conditions. The company's adjusted EBITDA soared to $50.2 million in the quarter, representing a 26.2% margin, jumping 960bps versus a 16.6% margin in the year-ago quarter.

The company here benefited from its decision to cut ~20% of its headcount in October, while also updating its pricing and improving its take rates on its GSV. And yet we note that the fiscal discipline that Upwork is showcasing for its own workforce may be exactly the trend that other companies are replicating, which is directly impacting Upwork's ability to advance its GSV.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $17, Upwork trades at a market cap of $2.22 billion. After we net off the $622.1 million of cash and $357.9 million of debt on Upwork's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.96 billion.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year FY25, Upwork has guided to $740-$760 million in revenue, which represents a decline of -1% to -4%, and adjusted EBITDA of $180-$190 million - or a 24.7% margin at the midpoint that represents 10% y/y growth.

As such, Upwork trades at 10.6x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. The stock is neither cheap nor expensive: but we have to note that this valuation multiple reflects a company that is expecting declines in GSV and revenue, as well as long-run risks stemming from AI that may make GSV growth quite difficult to achieve at all, even when macro conditions subside.

To me, this is not the right stock to bank on: steer clear and invest elsewhere.
